summary: Today's sweep leans toward Games / criticism, Democracy / press freedom, CS education / AI policy. The strongest items in this run are California bill pushing to keep games playable after server shutdowns "doesn't reflect how games actually work", ESA assert, Students Boo Commencement Speaker After She Calls AI the ‘Next Industrial Revolution’, and Former Epic tech director announces The Immense Engine, his AI-heavy "European alternative" to Unreal.

mainItems.title: California bill pushing to keep games playable after server shutdowns "doesn't reflect how games actually work", ESA assert
mainItems.summary: Earlier this month, Stop Killing Games, the campaign group seeking to prevent online games being rendered unplayable when publishers shut down their servers, threw their support behind a Californian bill aiming to put a requirement that…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: Students Boo Commencement Speaker After She Calls AI the ‘Next Industrial Revolution’
mainItems.summary: A commencement speaker at the University of Central Florida was booed, with graduating humanities students yelling out, "AI SUCKS!"
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, student agency, and school policy.

mainItems.title: Former Epic tech director announces The Immense Engine, his AI-heavy "European alternative" to Unreal
mainItems.summary: Guerilla Games co-founder and former Epic Games technical director Arjan Brussee is making what he styles a "European alternative" to US-developed videogame engines like Unreal and Unity (the latter originally conceived in Denmark, to be…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth your time if you care about design, criticism, and not just hype.

mainItems.title: This week in PC games: Subnautica 2 early access, a Battlestar Galactica roguelite, and Directive 8020 disproving the old saying about space and screaming
mainItems.summary: Our infamously workshy news editor Edwin – he only posted barely twice the number of articles last week as I did, the skiver – is off doing something that isn’t the Maw this morning.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: ‘Truly terrifying’: Alberta voter data breach raises fears for Canada’s electoral integrity
mainItems.summary: Debates over secession overshadowed by revelations separatist-linked group gained access to list of electors The illegal use of voter information by rightwing separatists in the province of Alberta has raised fresh fears over Canada’s…
mainItems.whyItMatters: Worth watching for authoritarian drift, selective enforcement, and democratic norms.

mainItems.title: Sony's failed war against Internet piracy may doom other copyright lawsuits
mainItems.summary: Cable firm Cox's Supreme Court win may help all tech providers, not just ISPs.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Touches trust, surveillance, and the private use of AI systems.

mainItems.title: Making AI Education Accessible with CSTA Responsible AI Fellow Dan Jones
mainItems.summary: Daniel Jones is a CTE and computer science teacher for a large school district in South Florida. He also advises...
mainItems.whyItMatters: Directly relevant to teaching, classroom practice, and educational judgment.

mainItems.title: Forza Horizon 6 has been leaked and cracked a week before its release
mainItems.summary: The full version of Playground Games' upcoming Forza Horizon 6 game has leaked online over the weekend and has already been cracked by pirates.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to the shape of current AI systems and where the tooling is moving.

mainItems.title: Wealthy Students More Likely to Get Disability Accommodations, Study Finds
mainItems.summary: While intended as a universal benefit, educational support for disabled children is significantly segregated by class, according to a paper released in January.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to state power, civil liberties, and anti-authoritarian concerns.

mainItems.title: Several states considering ban on legal personhood for AI
mainItems.summary: Is it time to consider prosecuting AI models for breaking the law? Some say it's time. Others say that would be an insult to humanity. Some states are considering laws blocking AI personhood.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Interesting where engineering, science, and tech infrastructure collide.

mainItems.title: Your AI Use Is Breaking My Brain
mainItems.summary: AI writing is impossible to avoid, is making everything sound the same, and is driving us crazy.
mainItems.whyItMatters: Relevant to your current interest graph.

mainItems.title: As ranks of uninsured grow, charity care can be hard to come by at many hospitals

mainItems.title: Slay the Spire 2 beta patch cuts Act 3 boss Doormaker, introduces Bestiary
mainItems.summary: Along with numerous other tweaks and revisions, Slay the Spire 2's latest beta patch pulls Act 3 boss, Doormaker, and replaces it with brand new boss Aeonglass, as well as introducing a highly requested Bestiary. Read more
mainItems.whyItMatters: Useful if you care about how games actually work as designed experiences.

new.title: Scavengers Reign
new.why: Animated speculative sci-fi with strong worldbuilding and a weirder imagination than most prestige SF.

new.title: Sugar
new.why: Noir detective structure with a genre wrinkle and a sensibility that feels adjacent to your crime/strangeness overlap.

new.title: The Lazarus Project
new.why: Time-loop espionage, moral pressure, and speculative plotting — very plausible fit.

new.title: Bodies
new.why: Time-spanning conspiracy/mystery structure that fits your taste for long-arc puzzle storytelling.

new.title: Constellation
new.why: A little more cerebral and mood-heavy, but it scratches the identity / reality-slippage itch.

new.title: The Devil’s Hour
new.why: Dark, twisty, and structurally ambitious without feeling like homework.

older.title: Counterpart
older.why: Probably the single cleanest “James show you may have missed”: espionage + parallel-world sci-fi + adult competence.

older.title: Patriot
older.why: Dry, strange, melancholy spy storytelling with a very distinct voice.

older.title: Utopia
older.why: Paranoid conspiracy energy, formal boldness, and a willingness to get strange and ugly.

older.title: Rubicon
older.why: A low-key conspiracy thriller that leans heavily on intelligence work, paranoia, and institutional atmosphere.

older.title: Continuum
older.why: A cleaner old-school sci-fi fit if you want procedural momentum wrapped around time-travel politics.

older.title: Person of Interest
older.why: Starts procedural and then quietly becomes one of the smartest AI / surveillance shows on television.

shared.title: Bad Sisters
shared.why: Darkly funny, well-observed, and ensemble-driven in a way that plausibly overlaps with Hacks / Derry Girls / Mare of Easttown energy.

shared.title: Detectorists
shared.why: Gentle, funny, humane, and specific — a softer shared-watch possibility.

shared.title: Astrid et Raphaëlle
shared.why: French procedural with a strong central duo and exactly the kind of foreign-crime appeal Maggie may share with you.

shared.title: Somebody Somewhere
shared.why: A warmer character-driven recommendation if the shared overlap leans more Bear / Good Place than pure detective work.
